item.title: What Tighter Corn Acres Mean for Cattle Feeders in 2026
item.description:
Replace with your own analysis. The structure below shows how to embed R output.

The USDA’s Prospective Plantings report signals a [X]-million-acre shift in corn acreage this year. For cattle feeders, the downstream effects on corn prices, feedlot cost of gain, and break-even calculations are worth thinking through now — before spring placements.

The Supply Picture

[Your analysis here. You can pull USDA NASS or CME data directly in R:]

Embed your R analysis here.

Feedlot Break-Even Implications

At current feeder cattle prices and a corn price assumption of $[X]/bu., cost of gain works out to roughly $[X]/cwt. for a 150-day closeout. [Continue your analysis…]

What Cow-Calf Producers Should Watch

Feeder cattle demand at the sale barn is sensitive to feedlot margins. If corn stays elevated through summer, expect [your conclusion]…

Questions or comments? Email me.

item.title: Working Capital: The Number Your Lender Is Really Watching
item.description:
Replace with your own analysis.

After several years of strong cattle markets, some operations built up comfortable working capital cushions. But sustained input cost inflation and, in many areas, consecutive drought years have eroded those buffers. Lenders are watching this number closely heading into the 2026 operating loan season.

What Is Working Capital?

Working capital is current assets minus current liabilities. On a ranch balance sheet, current assets typically include:

  • Cash on hand and operating account balances
  • Accounts receivable (cattle sold but not yet paid)
  • Prepaid expenses (feed, fuel, supplies)
  • Feeder cattle and stocker inventory
  • Growing crops or hay inventory (if applicable)

Current liabilities include the operating loan balance, accounts payable, and the current portion of any term debt coming due within 12 months.

Embed your R analysis or visualization here.

The Working Capital-to-Gross Revenue Ratio

Raw working capital doesn’t mean much without context. A $200,000 cushion is comfortable for a 50-head cow operation and thin for a 500-head feedlot. The ratio to gross revenue scales for size:

  • Above 30% — strong position
  • 10–30% — adequate, monitor trends
  • Below 10% — early warning territory; lenders will ask questions

If Yours Is Trending Down

[Your practical guidance for cow-calf and stocker operators]

Questions? Email me.

item.title: Q4 Ag Credit Survey: What It Signals for Western Cattle Operations
item.description:
Replace with your own analysis.

The Kansas City and San Francisco Fed districts — the two most relevant to western cattle operations — both published their Q4 agricultural credit surveys last week. The headline numbers are mixed, with some improvement in loan repayment rates but continued softness in operating loan demand and a modest cooling of farmland values in drought-affected counties.

Repayment Rates

[Your analysis of the specific survey data]

Pull and chart Fed ag credit data with fredr + ggplot2.

Farmland Values in Drought Country

One notable pattern this quarter: [your analysis of farmland values in drought-affected western counties vs. more favorable areas]

Collateral and Operating Loan Terms

[Your analysis of collateral requirement and loan term changes]

What to Watch in 2026

[Your forward-looking commentary for cattle producers and ranchers]

This post reflects my own analysis and is not an official position of [University Name] or any Federal Reserve Bank.

Questions? Email me.
